title: Live Comments / Presence — System Design
description: Design the live comment + reaction layer for a stream (sports, a celebrity live, a launch): millions of viewers watch one room, comments and reactions flow in, and everyone sees a real-time feed. The themes are extreme one-to-many fanout, taming the comment firehose (sampling), approximate presence/reaction counts, and backpressure.

nodes.label: Viewer Client
nodes.description: Watches a live stream; posts and receives comments and reactions.

nodes.label: Connection Gateway
nodes.description: Routes viewer WebSocket connections to gateways.

nodes.label: WebSocket Gateway
nodes.description: Holds millions of viewer connections; pushes the live feed down.

nodes.label: Comment Ingest Service
nodes.description: Accepts posted comments; validates, rate-limits, moderates.

nodes.label: Per-Room Broadcast Bus
nodes.description: Ordered stream of a room's comments fanned to gateways.

nodes.label: Fanout Workers
nodes.description: Read the room bus and push to that room's gateways.

nodes.label: Room / Subscription Registry
nodes.description: Which gateways hold viewers of each room.

nodes.label: Sampling / Rate Controller
nodes.description: Caps the firehose: samples comments shown when volume is extreme.

nodes.label: Presence / Counter Service
nodes.description: Approximate live viewer counts and reaction tallies per room.

nodes.label: Moderation Filter
nodes.description: Synchronous allow/drop gate before broadcast; on timeout, fail-closed for new/low-trust users and async-quarantine the rest.

nodes.label: Quarantine / Review Queue
nodes.description: Holds rejected or slow-to-score comments for async human/heuristic review; nothing here reaches the bus.

nodes.label: Room Partitioner
nodes.description: Splits a mega-room into (room_id, slice_id) slices and assigns each slice to fanout workers/gateways under a room epoch.

nodes.label: Comment Store
nodes.description: Optional durable record / replay of comments (for VOD).

nodes.label: Reaction Aggregator
nodes.description: Aggregates high-volume reactions into counts, not per-event pushes.

functional: Viewers join a live room and receive comments in near-real-time.
Post a comment/reaction to the room.
Show an approximate live viewer count and reaction tallies.
Optionally persist comments for replay alongside the VOD.
nonFunctional: Extreme fanout: one room can have millions of concurrent viewers (1 comment -> millions of deliveries).
Low latency: comments appear within a second or two of posting.
Survive comment storms: when posting volume is huge, the system must not melt or overwhelm clients.
Best-effort delivery is acceptable (it's a live feed; missing some comments is fine).
Approximate counts are fine (exact live viewer count isn't required).
Safe + abuse-resistant: authenticated posting, per-user rate limits, retry-idempotent comments, and no unscreened content broadcast.
Operable: reconnect-tolerant, with metrics/SLOs for fanout lag, drop rate, sampled rate, and moderation/registry health.

capacity.label: Hot-room scenario
capacity.note: One mega-room at peak (a World Cup final). 50K reactions/sec on top.

capacity.label: Naive deliveries/sec
capacity.note: If every comment went to every viewer — the impossible number the design must avoid.

capacity.label: Gateways / viewers-per-gateway
capacity.note: 5M viewers ÷ ~1K connections/gateway. Hot-room viewers spread across gateways, not concentrated.

capacity.label: Upstream pushes (before sampling)
capacity.note: Hierarchical fanout already cuts 25B -> 25M by pushing once per gateway.

capacity.label: Upstream pushes (after sampling)
capacity.note: Cap the delivered rate to ~20 readable comments/sec/room; gateways multiply locally to their ~1K viewers.

capacity.label: Egress (sampled)
capacity.note: Borne at the gateway edge after local multiplication, not upstream.

capacity.label: Reactions / counts
capacity.note: Aggregation makes count cost independent of tap volume.

capacity.label: Bus partitions / latency / delivery
capacity.note: Live feel, not hard real-time; dropping/sampling comments is acceptable.

api.description: Join a live room and receive the broadcast feed. Carries auth/session, the current room epoch, an optional last-seen live sequence (best-effort resume — the server may skip ahead), client capabilities, and the desired feed mode (full vs sampled).
api.response: { "subscribed": true, "roomEpoch": 7, "sliceId": 3, "startSeq": 80432 }  // then streamed comments + count deltas

api.description: Post a comment. Authenticated (user id derived from session), idempotent on clientMessageId (retries return the same result), rate-limited per user. Moderation gates publication: only an allow verdict reaches the bus.

Send reactions, aggregated into counts rather than broadcast individually. Clients batch their own taps (count + window) to cut request volume; the server rate-limits per user and folds them into the room tally. Best-effort: dropped reaction batches only nudge an approximate count.

dataModel.name: room_subscription (live)
dataModel.note: Routing: which gateway holds a slice of a room. Lease/TTL + heartbeat lets the registry expire stale gateways after a crash. Keyed by (room_id, slice_id).

fields.name: gateway_id
fields.type: bigint (one row per gateway holding this slice)

fields.name: connection_count
fields.type: int (viewers on this gateway for this slice)

dataModel.name: comment (optional durable)
dataModel.note: Persisted only if replay/VOD comments are needed; idempotent on (room_id, client_message_id).

fields.name: retention_until
fields.type: timestamp (TTL for live; longer if kept for VOD)

dataModel.name: reaction_tally
dataModel.note: Aggregated reaction counts per room, bucketed by time window so deltas are pushable and versioned for freshness.

patterns.name: Per-room broadcast bus
patterns.what: Publish a room's comments to one ordered stream; gateways subscribe and push to their viewers.
patterns.whenToUse: One-to-many broadcast to many subscribers of the same channel.
patterns.group: Ingest and the Per-Room Broadcast Bus

patterns.name: Gateway fanout tier
patterns.what: Hold viewer connections on gateways and fan a single room message to all of them, not to each viewer individually upstream.

patterns.name: Sampling / rate capping
patterns.what: When comment volume exceeds what's readable, show a representative sample rather than everything.
patterns.whenToUse: Firehose rooms where full delivery is pointless and expensive.

patterns.name: Approximate aggregated counts
patterns.what: Aggregate reactions and viewer counts into approximate tallies pushed periodically.
patterns.whenToUse: High-volume counters where exactness isn't needed.

patterns.name: Best-effort delivery + backpressure
patterns.what: Drop/coalesce under load and protect clients/servers instead of guaranteeing every message.

patterns.name: Moderation gate (before broadcast)
patterns.what: Synchronously allow/drop each comment before it reaches the bus; quarantine rejects and fail closed for low-trust users when moderation is slow.
patterns.whenToUse: Public live comments where unscreened content must never broadcast.
patterns.group: Ingest and the Per-Room Broadcast Bus

steps.title: 1. Naive: Everyone Polls a Comments Table
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. A viewer posts a comment with an HTTP POST that writes a row to a comments table; every viewer polls (GET /rooms/{id}/comments?since=...) every couple of seconds to pull new rows. 'Broadcast' is just everyone reading the same table.

This is trivial and collapses immediately at scale. Put numbers on it: 5M viewers polling every 2s is ~2.5M reads/sec against the comments table — almost all returning the same handful of rows — before any write load, which no database survives. And it can't actually fan one comment out to millions of people in near-real-time: poll-driven delivery is both laggy and ruinously expensive, and a comment storm (thousands of posts a second) makes both the write and the read side melt.

So the baseline motivates the real design: decouple posting from delivery with a per-room broadcast bus, and push comments out through a gateway fanout tier (one send per gateway, local multiplication to its viewers) instead of having millions of clients hammer a database.

view.caption: The viewer client posts a comment to the Comment Ingest Service, which writes it straight to the Comment Store that every viewer then polls — no moderation, bus, or fanout yet.

steps.decisionPrompt: If 5 million viewers each poll the comments table every 2 seconds, what read rate does the database see — and how late are comments?
whyNow: Establishing the poll-the-table baseline first makes it obvious why a broadcast bus plus gateway fanout is required — you cannot serve one room's comments to millions by having them all read a database.

recap.after: Comments written to a table; every viewer polls it for new rows.
recap.newRisk: Millions of pollers melt the database with mostly-empty reads, and one comment can't be fanned to millions in real time; comment storms overwhelm both write and read paths.

traps.trap: Broadcasting comments by writing to a table and having viewers poll it.
traps.why: Millions of pollers per room create millions of mostly-redundant reads per second and still deliver comments seconds late — the DB becomes the bottleneck.
traps.instead: Publish each room's comments to a broadcast bus and fan out through a gateway tier that pushes once per gateway and multiplies locally.

steps.title: 2. Ingest and the Per-Room Broadcast Bus
description: Separate posting from delivery. Viewers post comments to an ingest service that authenticates the user, deduplicates retries by clientMessageId, and rate-limits per user. Moderation is a real gate: a comment only reaches the per-room broadcast bus if moderation returns allow. Rejected (or slow-to-score) comments go to a quarantine/review queue and never broadcast — this enforces the safety requirement rather than screening asynchronously while publishing anyway.

Moderation runs on a tight latency budget (say ~150ms). If the model is slow or unavailable, fail closed for new/low-trust users (hold them in quarantine) and let only already-trusted users through, so a moderation outage degrades to stricter filtering, not an open firehose.

The bus is what decouples cheap posting from expensive delivery: ingest stays fast, the delivery tier scales independently, and one ordered stream per room gives the delivery tier a single consistent thing to subscribe to.

Persistence is accepted-only and happens after the allow verdict: the comment store that powers VOD replay contains only comments that passed moderation. Rejected/quarantined content lives in the separate review queue under its own audit retention and access-control policy — it is never written to the replay store. The live path's source of truth is the bus, not a database write per comment, so persistence is optional and off the hot path.

view.caption: The viewer client posts to the Comment Ingest Service, which screens via the Moderation Filter; only allowed comments publish to the Per-Room Broadcast Bus and persist (accepted-only) to the Comment Store, while rejected ones go to the Quarantine / Review Queue.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why publish comments to a per-room stream instead of delivering them directly from ingest?

concepts.definition: One ordered stream per room that ingest publishes to and the delivery tier consumes.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decouples cheap posting from expensive fanout and gives consistent room ordering.
concepts.example: Comment -> moderate (allow) -> publish to room R's stream -> fanout reads it; reject -> quarantine.
concepts.group: Ingest and the Per-Room Broadcast Bus

whyNow: Decoupling ingest from delivery via the bus is the structural foundation; the fanout problem is solved against this stream.

recap.after: Authenticated, idempotent ingest with a moderation gate; allowed comments on a per-room ordered stream.
recap.newRisk: One room may have millions of viewers — delivering each comment to all of them is the hard part.

flows.name: Moderation gate: allow publishes, reject quarantines
flows.note: A comment reaches the bus only on an allow verdict. Rejected or slow-to-score comments are diverted to the quarantine queue and never broadcast; on a moderation timeout, ingest fails closed for low-trust users.

steps.title: 3. Massive Fanout via Gateway Tier
description: The core challenge: one comment must reach millions of viewers in a room. Viewers hold persistent WebSocket connections spread across many gateway nodes; a room registry tracks which gateways have viewers of each room.

Fanout is hierarchical: a comment on the room bus is read by fanout workers, which push it once to each gateway holding that room's viewers; each gateway then locally broadcasts to all its connected viewers of that room. So a comment is delivered to a gateway once, not per-viewer over the network upstream — the gateway does the local multiplication.

This gateway-tier fanout is what makes millions-of-viewers feasible: the upstream system sends O(gateways) messages, and each gateway fans out to O(its viewers) locally. Concretely, 5K comments/sec to a 5M-viewer room would be 25B deliveries/sec if done per-viewer upstream; with ~5K gateways it becomes 5K x 5K ≈ 25M upstream pushes/sec, and each gateway multiplies locally to its ~1K viewers. (Step 4's sampling then cuts the 25M further.)

view.caption: Fanout Workers consume the Per-Room Broadcast Bus, look up the room's gateways in the Room / Subscription Registry, and push batches to the WebSocket Gateway, which delivers comments to viewer clients.

options.name: Hierarchical gateway fanout (default)
options.description: A comment is pushed once to each gateway holding viewers of that room (found via a room registry); each gateway then locally broadcasts to its own connected viewers.
pros: Upstream cost is O(gateways), not O(viewers): one push per gateway, each multiplies locally to its viewers.
Persistent WebSocket connections give sub-second delivery to millions.
The room registry lets fanout target only gateways that actually hold the room's viewers.
cons: Stateful gateways hold millions of connections — connection placement and failover are real work.
The room registry must stay current as viewers join/leave and gateways die.
Local broadcast still amplifies inside a gateway, so a single huge room concentrates load.

view.caption: Fanout Workers consume the room stream, find the room's gateways via the Room / Subscription Registry, and push one batch to each WebSocket Gateway, which locally delivers to its viewer clients.

options.name: Direct per-viewer pub/sub subscription
options.description: Every viewer subscribes directly to the room's channel on the message broker, which delivers each comment to all individual subscribers.
pros: Conceptually simple: every viewer subscribes to the room channel and the broker delivers.
No custom gateway-tier fanout logic — the message broker does delivery.
Works well for small rooms where per-subscriber cost is negligible.
cons: The broker must track and push to 5M individual subscribers per room — O(viewers) fanout it can't sustain.
Hot rooms concentrate millions of subscriptions on a few broker partitions.
No place to do the local multiplication that makes huge rooms affordable.

view.caption: Each viewer client subscribes directly to the room channel on the Pub/Sub Broker, which pushes every comment per subscriber at O(viewers) cost.

options.name: Client long-poll / pull from a room log
options.description: Viewers periodically pull new comments since their cursor from a room log served by a stateless tier, instead of holding a pushed connection.
pros: Stateless serving tier — clients pull new comments since their cursor, easy to scale horizontally.
No millions of held connections; survives client churn trivially.
Naturally cacheable: many clients read the same recent window of the room log.
cons: Polling adds latency (comments arrive a poll-interval late) — worse for a live feed.
5M viewers polling a busy room re-create the read-amplification the design is trying to avoid.
Tuning the poll interval trades freshness against load with no good answer at this scale.

view.caption: The viewer client long-polls the WebSocket Gateway since its cursor, which reads the recent window from the Room Comment Log.

steps.decisionPrompt: A comment is posted to a room with 5M viewers. How does it reach all of them without 5M upstream sends?

concepts.definition: Push a room message once per gateway; each gateway broadcasts locally to its connected viewers.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reduces upstream fanout from O(viewers) to O(gateways); local multiplication scales.
concepts.example: 5M viewers across 5,000 gateways -> 5,000 upstream pushes, each gateway fans to ~1,000.

whyNow: Hierarchical fanout is the central scaling idea; recognizing the gateway-as-local-multiplier is what makes millions-of-viewers tractable.

recap.after: Comments fanned to millions via a gateway tier.
recap.newRisk: When thousands of comments/sec hit a huge room, even hierarchical fanout overwhelms clients.

flows.name: Hierarchical gateway fanout (one send per gateway)
flows.note: A comment on the room bus is pushed once to each gateway holding that room's viewers; each gateway then multiplies locally to all its connected viewers, so upstream cost is O(gateways), not O(viewers).

steps.title: 4. Taming the Firehose: Sampling
description: At peak (a goal in a World Cup final), comments arrive faster than anyone can read — thousands per second. Delivering all of them to every viewer is pointless (a human reads a few per second) and ruinously expensive.

Sample the stream: when volume exceeds a readable rate, show each viewer a representative sample rather than every comment. The rate controller caps the delivered rate per room to something readable — say ~20 comments/sec — which is what turns the 25M upstream pushes/sec from Step 3 into ~100K/sec (20 x 5K gateways) before local multiplication. The feed stays lively without flooding clients or the network.

Placement matters: sampling sits after moderation (only allowed comments are eligible) and before fanout, so the sampler reads the moderated bus and emits rate-capped batches that fanout delivers. Persist all accepted comments for VOD if needed, but deliver only the sample live. Keep the sample shared per room — a single capped stream everyone sees; per-viewer personalized samples (followed users, high engagement) multiply fanout work, so reserve them for a small bounded slice rather than re-sampling for every viewer.
The key insight: full delivery is neither needed nor feasible for a firehose room, so best-effort sampling is the right design, not a compromise.

view.caption: The Sampling / Rate Controller reads the moderated Per-Room Broadcast Bus and emits rate-capped batches; Fanout Workers push those to the WebSocket Gateway — sampling reduces the stream before fanout multiplies it.

options.name: Random rate-capped sampling (default)
options.description: When volume exceeds a readable rate, a rate controller drops comments uniformly at random so each delivered comment had an equal chance of selection.
pros: Caps delivered comments to a human-readable rate regardless of how fast the firehose runs.
Statistically representative: every comment has an equal chance, so the feed reflects the room.
Cheap and stateless — no scoring or ranking on the hot path.
cons: Good comments can be dropped purely by chance.
No notion of relevance: a viewer may miss replies from people they follow.
Pure randomness can feel arbitrary in a very fast room.

view.caption: The Sampling / Rate Controller reads the moderated bus and drops comments uniformly at random down to the cap; Fanout Workers push the rate-capped batches to the WebSocket Gateway.

options.name: Priority / quality-ranked sampling
options.description: Each comment is scored (followed users, engagement, verified status) and the highest-ranked ones win the limited per-room delivery slots.
pros: Surfaces the most relevant comments: followed users, high-engagement, or verified accounts win the limited slots.
Better perceived feed quality than random under the same rate cap.
Can personalize per viewer (show comments from people they follow).
cons: Scoring every comment adds CPU and state on the firehose path.
Personalization means the sample differs per viewer — far more fanout work than one shared sample.
Ranking can bias the feed and hide the long tail of ordinary viewers.

view.caption: The Priority Ranker scores comments off the moderated bus and the Sampling / Rate Controller keeps the top-K within the rate cap; Fanout Workers then push the rate-capped batches to the WebSocket Gateway.

options.name: Deliver everything (no sampling)
options.description: Every comment is fanned out to every viewer with no rate cap or sampler in the path.
pros: Every viewer sees every comment — no information is dropped.
Simplest delivery path: no rate controller or sampler to build.
Fine for small/medium rooms where volume is naturally readable.
cons: 5,000 comments/sec floods clients no human can read and saturates the network.
Fanout cost scales with raw comment volume, which is ruinous in firehose rooms.
Slow clients fall behind instantly, pushing the backpressure problem everywhere.

view.caption: Fanout Workers push every comment in batches straight to the WebSocket Gateway, which delivers them all to viewer clients with no sampler.

steps.decisionPrompt: A room gets 5,000 comments/second. Do you deliver all 5,000 to every viewer? What should they see?

concepts.definition: Delivering a representative, rate-capped sample when comment volume exceeds readability.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the feed usable and the system affordable in firehose rooms.
concepts.example: Cap to ~20 comments/sec shown; sample/prioritize from the flood.

whyNow: Sampling is the firehose-specific insight that distinguishes live comments from chat: you deliberately don't deliver everything.

recap.newRisk: Viewers also want counts (who's watching, reactions) — high-volume signals of their own.

steps.title: 5. Presence and Reaction Counts (Approximate)
description: Live UIs show a viewer count and reaction tallies (hearts streaming up). These are extremely high-volume and don't need exactness: a useful target is 'viewer count refreshed every ~2s, within a few percent', not just 'approximate'.

Reactions are aggregated, not broadcast individually: a reaction aggregator tallies them into time-windowed counts (e.g. '+1,240 hearts in the last 2s') pushed to the room periodically, instead of pushing each heart to millions. Viewer count comes from summing per-gateway connection counts in the room registry. Note that's concurrent connections, not unique viewers — for a true unique-viewer estimate use an HLL sketch bounded by a session/heartbeat window, so a connection that hasn't sent a heartbeat in N seconds drops out of the estimate.

This trades exactness for massive efficiency: each count is a single small broadcast every couple seconds carrying a window delta and an aggregation version, regardless of how many reactions arrived — so cost is independent of tap volume.

view.caption: Viewer clients send reaction events to the Reaction Aggregator, which tallies counts in the Presence / Counter Service that pushes periodic viewer counts and reactions to the WebSocket Gateway.

options.name: Aggregated approximate counts (default)
options.description: A reaction aggregator tallies hearts and viewer counts and pushes a single small count delta to the room every couple of seconds instead of broadcasting each event.
pros: One small count delta per room every couple seconds instead of a million heart events.
Cost is independent of reaction volume — a million taps cost the same broadcast as a thousand.
Exactness isn't needed for hearts/viewer counts, so approximation is correct, not a compromise.
cons: Counts lag by the aggregation interval — not instantaneous.
Approximate, so the number shown is never exactly right at any instant.
The aggregator is a per-room hot path that must itself scale with taps.

view.caption: Viewer clients send reactions to the Reaction Aggregator, which tallies them in the Presence / Counter Service that pushes a small periodic count delta to the WebSocket Gateway.

options.name: Exact per-event reaction broadcast
options.description: Every individual reaction is fanned out to every viewer as its own event so counts and animations are exact and instantaneous.
pros: Every heart animates instantly for every viewer — maximally live feel.
No aggregation lag and no approximation in the displayed count.
Simple semantics: one tap, one broadcast event.
cons: A million taps means a million fanouts — the exact O(viewers) blowup the design avoids elsewhere.
Saturates gateways and the network for a signal nobody reads precisely.
Provides no benefit over an approximate count for a streaming-hearts UI.

view.caption: Each viewer client's reaction goes to the Reaction Aggregator, which broadcasts every tap to the WebSocket Gateway for exact per-event delivery to viewer clients.

options.name: Probabilistic sketch for viewer count (HLL)
options.description: Each gateway keeps a HyperLogLog sketch of its unique viewers; a global count is the cheap union of those mergeable sketches.
pros: HyperLogLog estimates unique viewers in kilobytes regardless of room size.
Mergeable across gateways, so a global count is a cheap union of per-gateway sketches.
No need to hold or count every connection centrally.
cons: Only an estimate (a few percent error) — wrong if exact counts are ever required.
Mainly suits the unique-viewer count, not reaction tallies.
Adds a sketch data structure and merge logic to the presence path.

view.caption: The WebSocket Gateway adds connection ids to a per-gateway HLL Sketch, whose merged estimate feeds the Presence / Counter Service that reports the viewer count to gateways.

steps.decisionPrompt: A million viewers tap the heart button at once. Do you broadcast a million reaction events?

concepts.definition: Tally high-volume reactions/viewers into approximate counts pushed periodically.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids per-event fanout for signals where exactness doesn't matter.
concepts.example: Aggregate hearts; push a count delta every 2s instead of each tap.

whyNow: Counts are a second high-volume fanout; aggregating-then-broadcasting (not per-event) is the parallel insight to comment sampling.

recap.newRisk: Under extreme load, the system must protect itself and degrade gracefully.

flows.name: Aggregate reactions into a periodic count push
flows.note: Individual reaction taps are tallied by the aggregator, not broadcast one-by-one; a single small count delta is pushed to the room every couple of seconds.

steps.title: 6. Backpressure and Graceful Degradation
description: Live events spike unpredictably. Because delivery is best-effort, the system degrades by dropping/coalescing rather than buffering unboundedly or failing. Apply backpressure at each tier: ingest rate-limits posting, the sampler caps delivery, gateways drop/coalesce if a slow client can't keep up.

A slow or disconnected viewer must not back up the gateway. Make the policies concrete: a per-connection send buffer bounded at, say, ~256 messages; on overflow drop-oldest for comments (newest comments matter more on a live feed) but coalesce for counts (keep only the latest tally). Coalesce count pushes to one per ~2s. The room bus keeps a bounded retention window (e.g. last ~30s — it's a live feed, not durable chat) so it never grows without limit. If a connection's buffer stays full past a threshold, disconnect it and let the client reconnect rather than letting one laggard hold memory.

The principle: under overload, shed load cleanly (fewer comments shown, coarser counts) so the experience degrades gradually for everyone instead of the system collapsing — and emit metrics (drop rate, buffer depth, coalesce ratio) so shedding is observably deliberate, not silent loss.

view.caption: The Sampling / Rate Controller caps the Per-Room Broadcast Bus and Fanout Workers push capped batches to the WebSocket Gateway, which drops-oldest / coalesces into bounded per-connection buffers before delivering to viewer clients.

steps.decisionPrompt: A viewer on a bad connection can't keep up with the feed. What happens to their stream and the gateway?
whyNow: Backpressure + best-effort is what keeps a live system alive under unpredictable spikes; it's the resilience crux for a firehose.

concepts.definition: Bounded buffers that drop/coalesce under load instead of buffering unboundedly.
concepts.whyItMatters: Protects the system and other viewers from one slow consumer or a spike.
concepts.example: Per-connection buffer caps; drop-oldest comments for a lagging client.

recap.newRisk: Gateways and rooms must scale and survive failures at event scale.

steps.title: 7. Scaling Rooms and Gateways
description: Scale the gateway fleet by connection count; the room registry is rebuildable from gateways re-registering (each entry carries a lease/TTL, so stale gateways expire after a missed heartbeat). A mega-room (one stream, millions of viewers) is the hard case: its bus and fanout must scale by partitioning the room into slices.

Concretely, a room partitioner splits a mega-room into (room_id, slice_id) slices and assigns each slice its own fanout workers, recording the ownership in the registry. The single sampled comment stream is replicated to every slice, so each slice delivers the same shared sample to its viewers; reactions tally per slice and roll up to a room total. This works because sampled live comments need only per-slice (event-time) ordering, not a global total order across slices — which is what makes slicing safe. A room epoch versions each assignment: when slices are reassigned, the epoch bumps and gateways/workers ignore stale-epoch pushes, so reassignment never duplicates or drops delivery.

Most rooms are small (slice 0 only); a few are enormous — provision elastically and route mega-rooms to dedicated capacity. Prewarm for scheduled big events. Comment persistence (for VOD replay) is async and off the live path.

Gateway failure is a brief reconnect: viewers reconnect to another gateway and re-subscribe to the room/slice; since delivery is best-effort, they simply resume the live feed (no catch-up needed). Presence/counts re-derive on reconnect.

view.caption: Control plane (dashed): the Room Partitioner writes (room_id, slice_id) assignments to the Room / Subscription Registry and hands slice ownership to Fanout Workers. Data path: the Sampler rate-caps the Per-Room Broadcast Bus and Fanout Workers read current slice assignments from the registry to push per-slice to the WebSocket Gateways behind the Connection Gateway.

steps.decisionPrompt: A single live room has 10M viewers. How do you fan out to all of them, and what happens when a gateway dies?

deepDives.title: Partitioning one mega-room across fanout capacity
points: Control plane, not hot path: the partitioner decides (room_id, slice_id) assignments and writes them to the registry; fanout workers and gateways read the current assignment. No comment batch flows through the partitioner per message.
Slice key (room_id, slice_id): viewers are assigned a slice at connect, and the registry maps each slice to its gateways — so no single bus/worker/gateway owns all 10M.
One shared sample, replicated: sample once per room, then fan the same rate-capped stream to every slice — don't re-sample per slice (that diverges the feed) or per viewer (that explodes fanout).
Ordering: per-slice event-time order is sufficient; global total order across slices is not required for a best-effort live feed, which is what makes slicing safe.
Room epoch: reassigning a slice bumps the epoch; workers/gateways drop stale-epoch pushes so reassignment doesn't duplicate or miss messages.
Counts: each slice tallies locally and the presence service rolls slice tallies into an approximate, time-windowed room total.

deepDives.title: What to put on the dashboard (SLOs & shed signals)
points: Fanout lag per (room, slice): time from bus publish to gateway push — the primary live-feel SLO.
Gateway send-buffer depth and per-connection drop rate, so load-shedding is observably deliberate, not silent loss.
Sampled delivery rate vs. cap and the raw comment rate (the sampling ratio that proves the firehose is being tamed).
Reconnect rate and registry staleness (entries past their lease/TTL).
Moderation decision latency and quarantine backlog; counter freshness (age of the latest pushed count window).

whyNow: Closing on mega-room scaling + gateway failover shows the design handles the extreme case (one giant room) and degrades gracefully.

recap.after: Elastic gateways, partitioned mega-rooms, rebuildable routing, reconnect-tolerant.
recap.newRisk: Mega-room fanout cost and prewarming — bounded by partitioning, elastic scaling, and best-effort semantics.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Partition into (room_id, slice_id) slices across dedicated fanout workers/gateways; replicate one shared sample to each slice; prewarm for scheduled events.
Thin gateways + lease/TTL room registry + client reconnect (resume live, no catch-up).

bottlenecks.issue: Stale registry entries after a gateway dies.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Lease/TTL + heartbeat: an entry that misses heartbeats expires, so fanout stops pushing to a dead gateway slice.
Bounded moderation latency budget; fail closed for low-trust users; quarantine overflow for async review rather than letting unscreened comments through.

failureDrills.scenario: A gateway holding many viewers of a mega-room crashes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Those viewers reconnect to other gateways and re-subscribe to their slice; they resume the live feed (best-effort, no replay).
failureDrills.mitigation: Lease/TTL registry expires the dead gateway + reconnect; best-effort delivery means no catch-up burden.

failureDrills.scenario: Fanout lags on one room slice while others are healthy.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: That slice's viewers see a slightly staler/thinner feed; other slices and rooms are unaffected.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-slice isolation + autoscale that slice's workers; shed via the sampler cap before lag grows.

failureDrills.scenario: Moderation backlogs during a goal-moment spike.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Comments from trusted users still flow; new/low-trust comments are held (pending_moderation) rather than broadcast unscreened.
failureDrills.mitigation: Latency budget + fail-closed + quarantine; alert on quarantine backlog growth.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Live Comments & Presence
finalDesign.description: Viewers hold WebSocket connections to gateways (a lease/TTL room registry tracks which gateways hold each room slice). Posted comments are authenticated, deduplicated by clientMessageId, and rate-limited at ingest; moderation is a real gate — only an allow verdict reaches the per-room broadcast bus (and the accepted-only replay store), while rejects and slow scores go to a quarantine queue and a moderation timeout fails closed for low-trust users. The sampler reads the bus and emits rate-capped batches; fanout workers push each batch once per gateway holding that room's viewers and each gateway broadcasts locally (O(gateways) upstream, local multiplication). Reactions and viewer counts are aggregated into approximate, time-windowed tallies pushed periodically rather than per-event. Delivery is best-effort with backpressure (bounded per-connection buffers, drop-oldest comments / coalesce counts); a room partitioner (a control-plane component, not on the per-comment path) slices mega-rooms into (room_id, slice_id) under a room epoch and writes the assignments fanout reads, the registry is rebuildable, and gateway failures are brief reconnects that resume the live feed.

view.caption: Viewer clients connect through the Connection Gateway to WebSocket Gateways tracked in the Room / Subscription Registry; comments flow through the Comment Ingest Service and the Moderation Filter (allow -> bus + accepted-only Store, reject -> quarantine), then the Sampler rate-caps the Per-Room Broadcast Bus and Fanout Workers push to gateways reading current slice assignments. The Room Partitioner is control plane (dashed): it writes (room_id, slice_id) assignments to the registry and hands slices to fanout, off the per-comment path. Reactions aggregate into presence counts.

functional.how: Screen before publishing to the bus.
Async durable comment store off the live path.

nonFunctional.how: Drop/coalesce under load; reconnect resumes live.
Auth + per-user rate limit + idempotent clientMessageId; moderation gate before broadcast with fail-closed.
Lease/TTL registry, room epochs, and SLO metrics (fanout lag, drop/sampled rate, moderation/registry health).

say: Confirm one-to-many: millions of viewers per room, best-effort live feed.
Pin extreme fanout and storm survival as the goals.
Establish that approximate counts and dropped comments are acceptable.
Name safety up front: authenticated posting, per-user rate limits, and no unscreened content broadcast.
Authenticated, idempotent (clientMessageId) ingest -> moderation gate (allow -> bus, reject -> quarantine) -> per-room broadcast bus.
Sampler reads the moderated bus and emits rate-capped batches before fanout (Bus -> Sampler -> Fanout).
Approximate aggregated reaction/viewer counts (time-windowed; concurrent connections vs unique viewers).
Backpressure and best-effort dropping with explicit shed policies.
Mega-room slicing as a control-plane assignment (room epochs), gateway failover, and lease/TTL registry recovery.
Tradeoffs: sampling rate vs completeness, count accuracy, dedicated mega-room capacity.
Operability and safety: moderation fail-closed, idempotent retries, lease/TTL registry, room epochs, and SLOs (fanout lag, drop rate, sampling ratio, moderation backlog, counter freshness).
Mention smart sampling and replay-for-VOD as extensions.

expectations: Uses WebSockets and fans out comments.
May try to deliver everything and miss sampling/backpressure.
Designs gateway-tier hierarchical fanout + per-room bus, sampling before fanout.
Best-effort + backpressure; moderation as a real gate with idempotent ingest.
Reasons about O(gateways) vs O(viewers) fanout precisely.
Designs mega-room slicing as a control-plane assignment (room epochs) and bounded-buffer backpressure.
Discusses sampling strategy and approximate counts deliberately.
Operates under event spikes with explicit shed signals and SLOs: fanout lag, gateway buffer depth, drop/sampling ratio, registry staleness, moderation backlog, and counter freshness.

followUps: How would you make sampling smarter (prioritize followed users / high engagement)?
How do you partition a single 10M-viewer mega-room across fanout capacity?
How do you keep viewer counts approximately right without exact tracking?
How would you persist comments for synchronized replay on the VOD?
How do you prevent one slow client from degrading a gateway's other viewers?

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: The Tail at Scale
